L2F: Learning to Focus on
Logical Regions for High-Resolution
GUI Grounding

A semantically-guided annotation framework teaching GUI agents to perceive interfaces through functionally coherent, window-aware logical regions.

Explore Annotation
The Challenge

Why Traditional Cropping Fails

Conventional cropping treats GUI as a flat pixel grid. When multiple similar elements exist across overlapping windows, models get hopelessly confused.

INSTRUCTION
Same task for both methods: “Close the File Explorer window.”
❌ Traditional Local Cropping

Coarse Localization → Ambiguous Crop

Model roughly locates the target area, expands a fixed bbox around it — but the box captures close buttons from multiple windows. Prediction fails.

Multi-Window Desktop
✅ L2F — Logical Region (WLR)

Window Lock → Precise Grounding

Model first identifies the containing window (WLR), then searches only within it. Single unambiguous close button found instantly.

Multi-Window Desktop
Annotation System

Three-Level Annotation Strategy

L2F covers every GUI type through three complementary levels. Distribution: WLR ≈ 50% · WSLR ≈ 40% · ELR ≈ 10%

WLR

Window-Level Logical Region

Multi-window environments · Task: "Save the file open in WPS"

For multi-window desktops: identify the specific application window containing the target, annotate that window's entire boundary as the logical region.

  • ①
    Locate the window containing the target
  • ②
    If window > 600×600: sub-divide within it
  • ③
    Occluded window → annotate full visible portion
  • ④
    Small window → expand, avoid contrast boundaries
WSLR

Workspace-Level Logical Region

Single-window multi-panel · Task: "Debug in VSCode"

For single-window apps: segment the interface into functional panels, annotate the workspace panel where the target task point resides.

  • ①
    Identify all functional panels within the window
  • ②
    Panel < 600×600: expand away from unrelated areas
  • ③
    Strong color-contrast boundary → don't cross it
  • ④
    Elongated panel → proportional expansion
ELR

Element-Level Logical Region

Simple / sparse interfaces · Task: "Turn off the clock at 4:30 pm"

For simple, sparse interfaces with no distinct workspace subdivisions. Centers the target element, minimizes distracting context.

  • ①
    No distinct workspace panels → use ELR
  • ②
    Center the target element in the region
  • ③
    Minimize surrounding context clutter
  • ④
    Expand slightly if region < 600×600
Supplementary · Section 4.1

Detailed Case Studies

Explore concrete annotation examples for each strategy. Click a case button to view the screenshot and detailed explanation.

🟡 WLR — Find the window that contains the target, then decide annotation scope
WLR Case ① — Multi-window desktop
Task Instruction
"Go back to the previous page from the visual overview of GNOME page."
🪟 Multi-window overlap → annotate the entire occluded window
🔍
Multi-window Overlap
Multiple windows overlap on the desktop. The back-button target lies inside a partially occluded GNOME window. The annotator first identifies which window contains the target — the one that is partially hidden beneath others.
🟡
WLR Decision
The entire occluded window is annotated as the logical region. Preserving the full window context eliminates cross-window ambiguity — a tight crop around the back button alone would risk capturing controls from other overlapping windows.
WLR Case ② — Overlapping windows
Task Instruction
"Install the Docker."
📐 Large window → decompose and annotate the relevant sub-region
📐
Large Window Decomposition
The target window (a Software Center / app store) is an independent, sufficiently large window with distinct functional areas — a left sidebar, an extension/app list, and action controls. WLR-style decomposition is applicable to identify its functional sub-regions.
🟡
WLR Decision
Among the identified sub-regions, the annotator selects the most relevant sub-area containing the "Install" button as the logical region — rather than annotating the entire large window — to provide a focused yet context-preserving bounding box.
WLR Case ③ — Window with taskbar
Task Instruction
"Setting the power."
🔲 Decomposable but size-limited → keep the full window
🖼️
Decomposable but Size-Limited
The Settings window has a decomposable structure — a left sidebar for navigation and a right content area for settings options. In principle, either could be used as a sub-region. However, the overall window size is limited, and further reduction risks losing important contextual information needed to identify the power setting.
🟡
WLR Decision
Because further subdivision risks losing critical UI context, the annotator retains the full window as the logical region. The window boundary itself already provides a well-defined, unambiguous scope for locating the target control.
WLR Case ④ — Complex multi-window layout
Task Instruction
"Minimize the Ubuntu Desktop interface."
🎨 Small window near a sharp color boundary → no expansion
📦
Small Window — Boundary Expansion Check
The target window (Ubuntu Desktop app) is relatively small, potentially suggesting moderate boundary expansion to meet a minimum region size. However, the annotator must check whether any expansion would cross a visually distinct boundary.
🟡
WLR Decision
Expanding the window boundary would cross a sharp color discontinuity — a white-to-dark-pink transition visible at the edge. To avoid introducing irrelevant content from a visually different region, expansion is halted. The original window boundary is retained as-is.
🔵 WSLR — Identify the workspace sub-region, then expand if too elongated or too small
WSLR Case ① — IDE / editor workspace
Task Instruction
"View terminal output."
📏 Elongated sub-region → expand toward fewer distractors
🔵
Elongated Sub-region
The terminal output panel at the bottom of the IDE forms an elongated horizontal strip. Annotating only this narrow strip would degrade model generalization — on wide images, the model might learn to include excessive background in a similar elongated fashion, hindering accurate target localization.
↕️
Directional Expansion
The initial tight bounding box (blue) is too narrow vertically. The annotator expands toward the direction with fewer distractors — upward into the code editor area — since the sidebar and activity bar on the left introduce more visual noise than the editor.
🟢
WSLR Decision
The expanded yellow region captures both the terminal panel and part of the code editor above it, creating a well-proportioned bounding box. This avoids excessive background interference and provides a richer, yet focused context for the model to localize the terminal output area.
WSLR Case ② — Multi-panel application
Task Instruction
"Add Travel 2023 to \"I Like\"."
🖼️ Image boundary constraint → expand inward instead
🔵
Image Boundary Constraint
The target sub-region (the top toolbar area of an application like a music/photo manager) is elongated along the top edge. Expansion toward the top is physically constrained by the image boundary — there is no space left to expand further in that direction.
🟢
WSLR Decision
When expansion is constrained by the image boundary, the annotator prioritizes a clean, well-defined region edge and avoids expanding into complex or cluttered neighboring areas. The expanded yellow region is bounded by the image top while expanding downward into a cleaner area to form a proper bounding box size.
WSLR Case ③ — Dashboard / property panel
Task Instruction
"Choose the small square checkbox next to the text 'All day'."
🔲 Small + low-contrast sub-region → moderate expansion for context
🔵
Small Low-Contrast Sub-region
The target (a small "All day" checkbox in a calendar event editing panel) is contained within a small sub-region that has low visual contrast with the surrounding calendar grid. A tightly cropped bounding box would be both too small and difficult to distinguish from neighboring cells.
🟢
WSLR Decision
To enhance contextual consistency and improve detection robustness, the bounding box is moderately expanded beyond the tight panel boundary. The expanded yellow region provides richer visual context for the model, while remaining clearly bounded within the calendar application window — ensuring the target is distinguishable from the background.
🟣 ELR — No functional panels detected — annotate a tight box centered on the target element
ELR Case ① — Calendar / Alarm screen
Task Instruction
"Navigate to the previous day."
🎯 No functional panels → tight box centered on target element
🟢
Element-Level Annotation
The calendar app interface does not have distinct functional workspace panels — the whole screen is dominated by a date-navigation calendar view. ELR applies directly: the annotator draws a tight bounding box centered on the "previous day" navigation button (a small arrow icon in the upper-right area).
🎯
Centering Rule
ELR annotation prioritizes minimizing interference from surrounding calendar grid elements. The primary objective is to ensure the target navigation button is centered within the annotated region, providing a focused and uncluttered context for fine-grained localization — without the calendar date cells causing confusion.
ELR Case ② — Map application
Task Instruction
"Click the \"GREENWICH\"."
🗺️ Sparse map interface → tight box centered on text label
🟢
Sparse Map Interface
The map application interface consists predominantly of a map canvas background with sparse, overlaid UI controls and place labels. There are no distinct functional workspace panels — the target "GREENWICH" label is an isolated text element on the map canvas. ELR is the appropriate strategy.
🎯
Centering Rule
ELR annotation centers the bounding box tightly on the "GREENWICH" label. The primary objective is to minimize interference from surrounding map content (streets, other place names, map tiles). The target element is isolated with limited surrounding visual interference — a focused region directly centered on it provides the clearest localization context.
Design Constraint

Region Size: 600×600 → 1024×1024 px

Ablation confirms 784×784 as the optimal crop size. The Regional-Constraint Reward R_C encodes this directly into RL training.

512×512
Below min
600×600
Min bound
784×784
Optimal ⭐
1024×1024
Max bound
1600×1600
Exceeds max

Avg. accuracy (%) on ScreenSpot-Pro by region crop size · Sideal = 784×784 = 614,656 px

Regional-Constraint Reward RC
R_C = exp( −(S_pred − S_ideal)² / 2(Λ₀ · S_ideal)² )

Gaussian penalty. Λ₀ = 0.25. Reward → 1 when predicted area ≈ Sideal; decays exponentially as deviation grows.

Why Constraints Matter

Without area constraints, models exploit trivial strategies — predicting the whole image as "logical region" satisfies inclusion rewards without providing grounding benefit. The Gaussian penalty forces genuinely focused region predictions.

Dataset Composition

Annotation Distribution & Data Quality

13K
Samples
WLR · Window-Level
≈ 50% · Multi-window desktops
WSLR · Workspace-Level
≈ 40% · Single-window multi-panel apps
ELR · Element-Level
≈ 10% · Simple / sparse interfaces

Low-repetition: Only 2.4 instructions per unique screenshot vs ~50 in comparable datasets. Each training iteration exposes a distinct visual layout.

Uniform spatial distribution: Task targets spread uniformly across all screen regions — unlike UI-Vision (80% upper-left), OS-ATLAS, ShowUI which concentrate in top portions.

🖥️

High-Resolution Priority

Min 1280×720. Most samples at 2880×1800, some at 3360×2100. Specifically designed for high-resolution grounding challenges.

🔁

Low Repetition Rate

2.4 instructions/screenshot vs ~50 in others. Maximizes visual diversity per training step, preventing memorization of specific layouts.

📐

Uniform Spatial Distribution

Rigorous filtering ensures task points are evenly distributed across the full screen space. Prevents positional bias toward UI hotspots.

🎯

Icon-Heavy Composition

Icon-to-text ratio ≈ 1:2. Intentionally elevated icon proportion strengthens symbolic visual reasoning — critical for professional software.

Annotation Pipeline

Annotator Decision Flow

1

Interface Assessment

Multi-window desktop? Single-window app? Sparse interface? Count windows and functional areas.

2

Level Selection

Multi-window → WLR. Single-window + distinct panels → WSLR. Sparse / no panels → ELR.

3

Bounding Box

Draw box containing the target. Verify: must include target point. Check: ≥600×600 px, ≤1024×1024 px.

4

Adjustment Rules

Too small? Expand carefully. Elongated? Balance proportions. Strong visual boundary? Don't cross it.

Experimental Results

Annotation Quality → Model Performance

L2F-trained models consistently outperform state-of-the-art baselines across all benchmarks and complexity levels.

+21.82%
↑ absolute gain
Maximum improvement of CogAgent-24 baseline on ScreenSpot-Pro when enhanced with P-LR plug-in model
50.86%
E2E-GG-7B overall
End-to-end model accuracy on ScreenSpot-Pro — surpassing prior SOTA with 89.6% relative gain over Qwen2.5-VL-7B baseline
+12.95%
avg. absolute gain
Average improvement across all tested baseline models on ScreenSpot-Pro when enhanced with the P-LR plug-in
Two Model Paradigms
P-LR Plug-in detection model — preprocesses screenshots by predicting logical region before passing to any downstream grounding model
E2E-GG End-to-end model — jointly predicts logical region and target point in a single forward pass using Logic-Progressive Prompt Strategy
Reward Function Design
R_total = R_ℓ · R_iou + w_C · R_C + w_p · R_p
R_ℓ: containment (target ∈ predicted box)
R_iou: region overlap with ground truth
R_C: area size constraint (Gaussian)
R_p: task-point accuracy (E2E only)

L2F: Learning to Focus on Logical Regions for High-Resolution GUI Grounding
